steps.title: Identify what the passage does with the two references
steps.content:
Alfonzo's core claim is that current understanding is "insufficiently robust"—incomplete. The passage then shows why: some studies emphasize demographic characteristics, others emphasize sidewalk width, and so on. The phrase "and so on" signals these are just two examples from a longer list. The function of both references is to illustrate piecemeal, siloed approaches that fail to capture the full complexity of walking decisions.
  • Choice A — 'They identify factors Alfonzo argues have been overemphasized...but that the text asserts are relevant to most people's walking decisions.': Alfonzo does not say these factors are overemphasized, only that focusing on any one factor is insufficient. The text also does not assert they are relevant to most people. ✗
  • Choice B — 'They represent factors that have been identified as important influences on walking decisions but that the text suggests are merely some of the many factors that may contribute.': Studies have flagged them as important, but Alfonzo's "insufficiently robust" judgment and "and so on" cast them as just two of many factors at play. ✓
  • Choice C — 'They are examples of factors studies suggest are important but that most people rarely consider when making walking decisions.': The passage says nothing about what individuals consciously consider; it focuses on what researchers have studied. ✗
  • Choice D — 'They illustrate factors people consider in most contexts but that the text argues are unique to Rome.': Demographic characteristics and sidewalk width are not presented as Rome-specific; Rome is mentioned only to introduce the problem of replicating walkability. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The references serve as evidence of fragmented research—each factor is recognized as important by some studies, yet none alone is sufficient, making them "merely some of the many factors." That matches choice B — B is correct.
